note_data.title: Waves (AS7)
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in a circle sector when arc length equals the radius
Angular speed ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is period, f is frequency
Linear speed v = ωr (product of angular speed and radius)
Objects in circular motion have constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, requiring centripetal acceleration
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to motion towards the center of the circle
Centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r using Newton's second law
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces
Newton's law of gravitation: F = Gm₁m₂/r² where force is proportional to product of masses and inversely proportional to square of distance
Gravitational field strength in radial fields: g = GM/r² following inverse square relationship

definitions.definition: The angle in a circle sector when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration of an object moving in circular path due to constantly changing velocity direction
The force that acts perpendicular to motion towards the center of the circle, causing circular motion
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give precise definition that matches mark scheme exactly
Show all working clearly with correct units and significant figures

modelAnswer.question: A sound wave has frequency 440 Hz and wavelength 0.75 m. Calculate the speed of sound and explain the Doppler effect when an ambulance approaches an observer. [5 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Speed calculation: v = fλ = 440 × 0.75 = 330 m/s. The Doppler effect occurs because there is relative motion between the ambulance (source) and observer. As the ambulance approaches, the observed frequency increases because the sound waves are compressed, resulting in a higher pitch. When receding, frequency decreases as waves are stretched, giving lower pitch.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct calculation v = 330 m/s with unit
Mark 3: States Doppler effect due to relative motion

commonMistakes: Forgetting that velocity changes in circular motion even at constant speed due to changing direction
Incorrectly applying inverse square relationship - if distance increases by factor of 2, field strength decreases by factor of 4
Not recognizing that centripetal force always acts toward the center, perpendicular to motion
Confusing radians and degrees when converting angles
Assuming gravitational field strength is constant everywhere when it varies in radial fields
examinerTips: Remember to convert between degrees and radians by multiplying by π/180 or 180/π respectively
For uniform spheres, treat mass as acting at the center when calculating gravitational forces on external objects
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Sinusoidal wave showing wavelength λ (distance between crests), amplitude A (maximum displacement), and direction of energy transfer. Include labels for crest, trough, and equilibrium position.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate wave speed using frequency and wavelength, then explain how amplitude affects energy transfer
Featured in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — multiple choice question on identifying transverse vs longitudinal waves with diagrams
Appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — students had to draw wave diagrams showing reflection at different boundaries and explain phase changes
Tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — comprehensive question covering wave equation, refraction calculations, and real-life applications of wave properties

workedExample.question: A water wave has a frequency of 0.5 Hz and travels at 2.0 m/s. Calculate the wavelength and explain what happens to the frequency and wavelength when the wave enters shallower water where the speed becomes 1.2 m/s. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Use wave equation v = fλ to find initial wavelength: λ = v/f = 2.0/0.5 = 4.0 m
Step 2: Recognize that frequency remains constant when waves change medium (f = 0.5 Hz)
Step 3: Calculate new wavelength in shallow water: λ = v/f = 1.2/0.5 = 2.4 m
Step 4: State that wavelength decreases from 4.0 m to 2.4 m while frequency stays at 0.5 Hz
workedExample.answer: Initial wavelength = 4.0 m. When entering shallow water, frequency remains 0.5 Hz but wavelength decreases to 2.4 m due to the reduced wave speed.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct wave equation, 1 mark for λ = 4.0 m, 1 mark for stating frequency constant, 1 mark for new wavelength calculation, 1 mark for λ = 2.4 m, 1 mark for explanation of change

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused amplitude with wavelength when describing wave properties' — always clearly label distance measurements on wave diagrams and specify what each represents

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently stated that wave speed changes but failed to explain why frequency remains constant during refraction' — remember that the source determines frequency, not the medium
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was drawing longitudinal waves as transverse waves' — longitudinal waves must show compressions and rarefactions, not up-and-down oscillations
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a transverse wave showing: wavelength (λ) as distance between consecutive peaks, amplitude (A) as maximum displacement from equilibrium position, clearly labeled peaks and troughs. For longitudinal waves: show compressions (particles close together) and rarefactions (particles spread apart) with arrows indicating particle motion parallel to wave direction. Include measurements and ensure wavelength is distance between equivalent points (peak to peak or compression to compression).
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion requires centripetal force toward the center due to constantly changing velocity direction. Angular quantities relate to linear quantities through radius relationships. Gravitational fields cause non-contact forces on masses, following Newton's law of gravitation with inverse square relationship. Field strength varies with distance in radial fields but remains constant in uniform fields.
